title: Chicken passanda
subtitle: Mild, creamy almond curry
excerpt: Chicken in a pale, rich sauce built from ground almonds, double cream, and yoghurt, lightly spiced with cardamom, cinnamon, and a small quantity of chilli for warmth rather than heat. Passanda is one of the mildest curries on the British Indian menu, with a sauce consistency close to korma but with the almond flavour more pronounced and the coconut absent.
sourceNotes: Passanda derives from the Mughal pasanda cooking style, which involved flattened, marinated meat (pasanda means 'that which pleases'). The British curry-house version became a mild cream-and-almond curry, listed alongside korma as an option for those who prefer little or no heat.

content.text: Passanda is a mild, soothing curry. The almond and cream make a sauce that is rich but not heavy. The key technique difference from korma is when the almond flour goes in. Here it cooks with the spices from early on. That develops the nuttiness of the almonds into the base, rather than just stirring them in at the end as a thickener.

The yoghurt must be added off the heat or at very low heat to prevent splitting. Full-fat yoghurt is more stable than low-fat; don't substitute. Once the yoghurt is in and the sauce has stabilised, the cream can go in over gentle heat without risk.

Heat the {{ghee}} in a wide pan over medium heat. Add the {{cardamom-green}} and {{cinnamon-stick}} and fry for 30 seconds. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 10 minutes until golden. Add the {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{turmeric}}, and {{chilli-powder}} and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{almond-flour}} and stir to coat, cook for 1 minute, stirring constantly. Add 200 ml water, stir until smooth, and bring to a simmer. Add the {{chicken-thighs-boneless}}, cover, and cook for 20 minutes.

Remove from the heat. Stir in the {{plain-yoghurt}} a spoonful at a time until incorporated. Return to a very low heat, add the {{double-cream}}, and stir gently for 3 to 4 minutes until heated through. Add the {{rose-water}} if using. Season with salt. The sauce should be pale, creamy, and coating the chicken. Remove the cinnamon stick and cardamom pods before serving.

items.fix: Remove from the heat completely before adding the yoghurt. Let the pan cool slightly, then stir in gradually. Return to the lowest heat setting for the cream stage
items.cause: Yoghurt or cream added over too-high heat

items.fix: The almond flour must be fried in the ghee with the spices for at least 1 minute before liquid is added, this is what develops the nutty flavour
items.cause: Almond flour not cooked in the spice base

items.fix: Add hot water 50 ml at a time, stirring between each addition, until the desired consistency is reached
items.cause: Almond flour absorbed too much liquid

items.fix: Reduce the double cream to 50 ml and add a little more water. The dish should still be creamy but not heavy
items.cause: Full cream quantity used for a lighter preference

content.text: Passanda sits in the same spot as korma in the British curry-house mild category, but it has a different character. The almond flavour is richer and more prominent than the sweetness-forward korma. It is a useful dish for groups with mixed heat tolerances. The rose water option, used in some versions, gives it a faint Mughal perfumed note. That sets it apart from other mild curries. The sauce is pale enough to show clearly on the plate, a visual signal of its mildness.
